speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1995. The survivor is David Jakubowski, J-A-K-U-B-O-W-S-K-I. And this is tape number 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, before discharge, they ask me where I want to go, because we were given tickets, free train rides. So I ask for a ticket to Radom. Why Radom? Because there was my aunt with the whole family, the sister of my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is after you got out of the POW camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we were on the train. To describe the ride, it was something unbelievable. There are hundreds of people-- smugglers that were getting in-- into-- in different small towns to get to Warsaw and sell. These were Poles. But finally we got to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I'm not going any farther. What we going to Radom? What will I do there in a small town? I stayed in Warsaw. I stayed in Warsaw, and I went-- I lived in Warsaw with my uncle, who was there with-- with my two cousins, the husband of my mother's sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: We all lived, four of us, in one room. Because you have to understand that in Warsaw every family was getting only one room to live. Doesn't matter how big they were. Because there was not much space.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't have any money. How we going to live in Warsaw? I went to a very famous gastroenterologist in Warsaw. His name was Dr. Plockier, P-L-O-C-K-I-E-R. Somebody told me to go to him because of his practice. So I went to him. I told him my story. Somehow he liked me, so he made me his assistant. Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. And I was going to him, visiting-- I mean, at that time we were doing house calls. On every house calls and if he prescribed any injections to give, I was giving the injections, and I was paid for this. And that how I lived during the time before the ghetto, naturally. Now this was in 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1940-- 1940. There were rumors in the-- in Warsaw the-- a ghetto will be formed. Now, there was a tremendous propaganda from the German side as far as the Jews are concerned. Because, you know, the Germans are a peculiar people. They wanted everything to be done legally.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were telling that the Jews are sick, horrible people, that they spread contagious disease to the poors. And therefore, they had to be put in the ghetto completely separate from the poors.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the Germans, we were their biggest enemies. We cause the First World War. We cause the Second World War. And therefore, they had the right to eliminate us, just like this. Now, on-- Warsaw was the main city in Poland. Before the war, there were 600,000 people living in Warsaw, of which around 200 or perhaps a little more were Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: 200,000 were Jews. Now, one day, on the 21st of July, a few cars of the Gestapo came to the ghetto and went to the so-called Judenrate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was the Judenrate? The name of it was, in the beginning, the Jewish Kehilla, which is a religious name-- a religious name. The German change it to Judenrate, which is a racial name. It's a very important difference. The chairman of it was-- of the Judenrate-- was Adam Czerniakow. He was an engineer. Nice guy, very nice-- I knew him very well-- short. But what could he do for the Jews against the Gestapo?

speaker: interviewee
text: His secretary general was Nachum Remba. He was a general Zionist, a fabulous guy. He was a very good friend of mine, and I lived with him for a while. His wife was a nurse. And I was working while there was no ghetto. Beside Plockier, I was going to the hospital, Jewish hospital in Warsaw. It was called the hospital on Czyste. This was one of the biggest Jewish hospitals in Europe, was many, many buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czyste? C-Z-Y-S-T-E. It was a complex of building, and I was working there on internal medicine. And there was working wife of Remba. She was a nurse. Now, so I mention Remba. I would like to mention also Emanuel-- Professor Emanuel Ringelblum, who was the historian of the ghetto. He was in a bunker in the ghetto, and there he was writing the history of the ghetto, what was happening, hour by hour. Now, he was killed-- I-- I'll come to it-- and all this notes, his notes, were put into 18 milk cans. 16 were found after the war. Two are missing. The 16 cans are in the Warsaw Jewish Museum. Now, the Judenrate was getting from the Germans all the orders, and it was their responsibility to do everything what they wanted. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They even organized in the ghetto a Jewish police. The commanding officer was Colonel Szerynski. The second one was a man Lejkin, a miserable guy, a gangster, who was walking around with a-- well, you call it a Peitsche in German. It was a whip, whipping people, Jews. But he was a private guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-E-J-K-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other man, the colonel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Colonel Szerynski. He was a officer in the Polish Army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish, yeah. There were a few.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-Z-E-R-Y-N-S-K-I. He had two lieutenants-- [? Schmeling ?] and [? Firstenberg. ?] Now, I must say that the Jewish police did not inscribe itself very favorable in the history of the ghetto in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we had to work. What the Germans were doing, they were looting the apartments. They were sending people out. Where? We didn't know. We haven't-- we have never seen them again. And every day they were on the streets and taking from the streets thousands of people to work outside the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to mention one more thing. I said that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on October 15, 1940, when they moved into-- to him to-- Czerniakow, the Gestapo, at the same time on almost every building in the ghetto showed up proclamations in German, which were-- which on it were written, [SPEAKING GERMAN], which means resettlement of the Jews to work in Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was on July 21. Try to remember this date, because I'll come back to it. At that day, the whole ghetto was just, by the Gestapo, they were committed to death, to die. On November-- on-- from October 1 until November 1, 150,000 Jews who lived on the Aryan side had to move in within the boundaries of the ghetto, and 80,000 Poles had to move out. Now, the Poles got-- the Poles got trucks. The Jews could take only what they could take on their shoulders, on a small pushcart. This was a madhouse. I was on the street, because I was going all over. I wasn't afraid of anything. And you could see on the street those old Jews with long gray beard, you know, pushing the cart. And the German soldiers-- kids, 18, 19, 20 years old-- turning them over, and those poor Jews screaming and crying, picking up these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what? Schmattas and [? putz. ?] This was a problem of the Jews in Poland. We were so attached to what we have that we were afraid to leave anything, even if we had a chance to go somewhere. Because the same did my parents. But we stayed in close to our-- to what we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: On December-- on November 1, 1940, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] everybody was supposed to-- supposed to be in. It was-- they brought Jews from different small villages around Warsaw. So there were around 500,000 Jews living in the ghetto, where there was space only for 200, 250,000 maximum. And then came the day, November 15, 1940, a day which I will never forget in my life. In the morning, I got out and tried to get to the point where I could get out of the ghetto and take a streetcar to my hospital on Czyste.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was closed. Nobody could get through. There was SS soldiers and the Polish police, and nobody could get out. So I remember I took a rickshaw. You know what a rickshaw is? It's a-- a rickshaw like in-- they use in China. Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a bicycle with seats in the front. I took a rickshaw around to another cross point. It was closed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In that-- during that morning, around 500,000 people who lived in the ghetto were all out on the street. And it's so-- it was so quiet that you could hear a fly. We knew one thing, that we were trapped, and the Germans could do with us whatever they wanted. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how was the life in the ghetto? It was a tough life, because the Germans gave us only a certain amount of food. For example, everyone-- it was divided by the-- by the Judenrate. They gave us 30 ounces-- 33 ounces of bread for a week, 3 and 1/2 ounces of marmalade for a month, 3 ounces of butter for a month. Why do I remember a thing like this? Because it sticks in your mind, you know, what was happening there. So how could the ghetto survive? It couldn't survive on this. Only by smuggling, smuggling the food from the Aryan side to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, there was a group. They called them Trzynastka in Polish, 13th. They lived on a street, Leszno, number 13. Across Leszno 13 was our hospital. They moved it from Czyste, and they gave us a huge building there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on-- on the Trzynastka, the main man was Ganzwajch, a gangster, who was running the whole smuggling. Now with him, some of the Germans were working together, and they made fortunes, him and the Germans. Now-- How do you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ganzwajch? G-A-N-Z-W-A-J-C-H. Now, for their memory, I have to mention here the children of the ghetto. They were dying simple from starvation. Every day at-- after the curfew-- it means after 6 o'clock-- we could see on the street a few kids, 7, 8, 9, 10 years old, dressed in old schmattas, rags, and almost crying in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH], so hungry, which means, throw down a piece of bread. We are so hungry. Please have pity on us. Now, they, those kids, were dying in their infancy. They grew old when they were small kids. There was no hand, no hand to give them any support where they were dying. And they were dying from starvation. And they were-- you could see when you walked-- I walked on the streets-- you could see the kids lying dead there beside grown-up people, dead, lying on the street. Because they were bringing down from the apartments to the street because they didn't have the money to pay for a funeral. I forgot to mention one thing, if I may. I said that the German was every day on the street and getting thousand of Jews to work for them on the other side, on the Aryan side. One day, I was on my way to the hospital on the Leszno Street. I was in my white coat with a band here, a Red Cross band. They caught me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why? I went with a big group to the Aryan side to work. I took my coat off first, because I didn't want them to know that I'm a physician. And we were unloading potatoes the whole day, potatoes from trains into sacks. And during the day, every-- almost every hour or hour and a half, one of them was coming, SS soldiers, says, we know there is a physician here. Where is he? I didn't say a word. In the evening, they let us go. And that what was going on in Warsaw almost every day. Why didn't you want them to know you were a physician?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I don't know what they want to do with me. I didn't know. They could have called me-- killed me, because they were killing, you know, the intelligentsia. I didn't know if they needed me to treat somebody or not. So I didn't want to take a chance. So I didn't give them even the chance. So in the evening, we went back to ghetto-- to the ghetto. Now, I finish about the children and how they were lying and about people who were lying. Now the dead people were lying on the street, died from starvation or from disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: The chevra kadisha, which is the burial society, were picking them up, putting them in the pushcarts, and taking to the very well-known, very old-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on end of Czyste was the-- what you call it in-- in English? Morgue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not morgue-- the morgue and then to the cemetery. One day, I decided to go myself to see how it looks like. At that time, I knew that my parents were already killed. This was in '41, in spring. They were killed in the ghetto of Kutno by-- in a big van. They were killed, my father, my mother, and my sister. So I said, what the-- I didn't have anything to risk. So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: They put them in a van?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a big-- big van and killed by the exhaust fumes. So I knew they were killed already. I never seen them, only once when I mentioned before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- where was I? I was interrupted.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were at the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, at the cemetery. First, I went to the mortuary where they kept the corpses. Now, you can't describe it. There were thousand of corpses, lying like on one above the other, thousands of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then I went out in the cemetery, there were mass graves. There were no funerals. They were throwing the corpses into the grave, hundreds of them. And that was it. I went there several time, because I wasn't really afraid of anything. What could happen to me? Kill me? Big thing. Who paid attention to it? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I mentioned before, on July 21, 1941, a group of Gestapo went to Adam Czerniakow. And at the same time, it was that put out all over the ghetto about a-- a settlement-- they call it resettlement of the Jews to work in-- in the East. Now, as I said before, they were so shrewd, the Germans. In the East?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the East.

speaker: interviewer
text: East of where?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all what they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said East Germany before.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, East.

speaker: interviewer
text: East, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The settlement of the Jews to the work-- to work in East. It means it was towards Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was East.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the dream, to take Russia, you know, East, for the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, you see, interrupt, I forget where I was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Oh, on the 21st, they were there. Now, they started there a settlement, what they call it, on July 22, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, Czerniakow committed suicide on July 23, two days after the Gestapo saw him. Why? We don't know. He didn't say a word, even to Remba, his general secretary and his best friend. So I don't know how to look at it. Heroism or cowardice? They let them to put him in a pushcart and to take him away to the cemetery on Czyste, and nobody was allowed to follow the pushcart. So it started on July 22. 6,000 Jews were [? supposed ?] to be delivered to the Umschlagplatz every day. Now, they were so shrewd, the Germans, that they gave the order to the chief medical officer of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was a meeting of the physicians, around 500, and the chief medical officer was Dr. Ganz, G-A-N-Z. And there was also with him another one. He was chief, too-- I don't know what kind of a chief-- Dr. Mielekowski. The names stick to me. And Ganz ask-- there were 500, a big room-- ask for a volunteer, because the German ask for a physician to have the first-aid station. God forbid that somebody get sick or get hurt that you have help right away. We didn't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a meeting. He ask for a volunteer. There was-- I wouldn't-- this-- this I never forget neither. One and a half or two minutes was you couldn't hear anything. And I got up. I volunteer. Because, as I said, I knew my parents were killed. So what--what can happen to me? Just get killed. So big thing. So-- so many killed during the ghetto that one more didn't mean anything. So it was-- you could hear, you know, such a relief, you know, from the people. And they gave me several papers, one in German-- in German was my photograph-- another one in German, and a Polish one. The German was for the German soldiers, the SS soldiers. The Polish was the-- for the Jewish police. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, I went to the Umschlagplatz. I was given 10 nurses. Put on white coat, Red Cross armband, and went to the Umschlagplatz. I was given there two rooms, one big room and one small room to organize a first-aid station. Now, Umschlagplatz, there is not even a word in English what Umschlagplatz means. This was a square, a huge square, with cars, train, car train, you know, and trucks, hundreds of them standing there. Now, my-- my-- my rooms were in a huge building, which was-- before the ghetto, it was infectious diseases hospital and [INAUDIBLE] the ghetto, too, and we had to clean it out within 24 hours. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So 6,000 Jews were brought daily to the Umschlagplatz. Now, during the time-- I was there three weeks-- I didn't see one patient. But unusual things-- unusual things happened to me in the-- in this-- in that station there.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day, the Oberscharfuhrer, SS Oberscharfuhrer, which is a sergeant, came to my-- to my room and ask me, who is in charge here? I said, me. What can I do for you? He says, give me the keys for the room across your-- your room, as you hid some Jews there, and the doors are locked. So you have the keys. Give me the keys.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, I give you one and a half to two minutes. If you don't give me the keys, I'm going to kill you, and put the machine gun right to my-- to my heart. I said, I'm sorry, I'm not-- I'm in charge only of these two rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: While in meantime, my girls, my nurses, were very smart. One run out. When they came in, one run out and brought with her Colonel Szerynski, the commanding officer of the Jewish Police, and one SS lieutenant. And he looked and he ask, what's going on? So he told him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said, I don't have any keys. Nobody's there. I am only responsible for these two rooms. So he said the two words-- [SPEAKING GERMAN]. It means, let the Jew live. This is something unforgettable, one of the miracles, which happened many to me, that I survive this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Boy, I have so much to tell.
